My family removed, when I was 5 yrs. old, from
Here we settled down & worked a farm for about 9 years (when I was nearly 14 yrs. of age), when we went to
& settled on a farm situated on a river called
Here we followed farming for several years. When I was about 18, (in 1820 or thereabouts), a party of 7 families started through the wilderness for
& I accompanied them, with a few cattle we took to sell, for the purpose of seeing
, & with the intention of going back for our family, if we found the country good.
Among our party there were about twenty men, including

& myself, all frontiersmen & a sufficient number to insure protection from the indian [Indian] tribes we might encounter. We had no adventure of note during our journey of over two months although we saw many parties of indians [sic] & passed through the country of
. They stole a few of our horses but his did not offer to trouble us in any other way.

About half way on our journey our bread gave out & we were obliged to live on meat the rest of the way. The whole country through which we passed was teaming filled with game, so that we never wanted for fresh meat. We had left
in the fall, about the month of Oct. & in December we reached
, a locality formerly visited by some members of our party, & where they had decided to make their homes. Two or three months later, however, they were obliged to move south of

& other Indians. They finally settled near or in the vicinity of

, & one Harril, the same family into which
sold his stock, consisting of hogs & cattle, to
, at a good bargain, & settled down on a farm just below

& settled a few miles from my brother Jacob's place.
in company with a young man by the name of
, who, like my self, fond of travel and adventure, & with a desire to see
, had determined to make a trip through that section of the country. We went to that this portion of
with his father & mother & several brothers & sisters who had come from
with us. They begged me to go with them but I had no desire to return at that time.
From 1822 until 1828 18[??] I made my home with my family. In 1827, I think,
, and I went up the Canadian fork of
& began the building of a large raft of cedar logs, which we intended to float down to
& sell. We were engaged, with a few men we hired, nearly a year in fmaking [making] this raft. When everything was ready & we were about to start down

to whom that section had been ceded, attempted to seize our raft, but taking advantage of a freshet we sailed down in the night beyond their reach, & escaped them. This availed us but little however for at the mouth of the fork, the raft ran aground[,] was broken up, & we abandoned it.
& myself to take to hunting & trapping, so in May of 1830 we joined a party of trappers & hunters that was forming just above

although he had been living for some time in

was at its head. He was an elderly man & by occupation a Gunsmith. It was thought He had never had any experience in hunting & trapping like that which was before us &, in fact, of our whole party of 48 men but three had fought the indians [sic] & none had ever trapped.
In those days the beaver were[,] it was supposed[,] plenty in the streams of
& the trappging of them, although attended with great risks owing to the indians [sic], is was largely engaged in by the frontier settlers.

was the first to propose the forming of a hunting & trapping expedition; & he had no trouble in raising from among the families in the vicinity our party of 48. Most All of our men could handle the rifle & like myself had been broughts [sic] up to the hunting of small game, although I had already, as some three others, become [...] a good deer & buffalo hunter. Before I was 9 yrs. old I began to use fire arms, & being very fond of hunting & shooting at a target I soon became an excellent shot. When I went with the party from

I had already become one of the crack shots at the annual shooting matches in the section where I lived, having but very few equals. This of course encouraged me to greater effort, & with the continual practice the then abundance of game then afforded me, I soon became one of the most successful hunters in that portion of the country. Among our party from

there were three of the best hunters detailed to supply the Camp with game meat, but notwithstanding they were good shots[.] I could always kill three times as much game as any of them, so that ere long I was appointed the chief hunter of the party. This piqued the old hunters not a little, who attempted to attribute the excellence of my shooting to my rifle, but, having exchanged guns with them from time to time, without any apparent change in my shooting, they finally concluded that I could beat them.

, had so much confidence in my shooting that he declared I could kill anything if I had only a corncob. I had also in after [sic] years become such a skillful buffalo hunter that on several occasions companies of trappers among
offered to bet as high as $5000 that I could kill more buffalo than any other man in

, my partner in the building of the raft, was a very good shot & buffalo hunter, having been raised on the Western Frontier. He had also done considerable indian [Indian] fighting. He could not travel much on foot, owing to the loss of the toes & part of the left foot, in what manner I do not recollect. He was then about 40 yrs. of age. A younger brother of about 30 also formed one of our party; he was no hunter & but a very ordinary shot.
I was as a general rule detailed to supply the party with fresh meat & as game abounded throughout our whole journey up

Each man was to equipp [equip] himself & hunt for himself, except that we were to keep together in moving about, for mutual protection, & or, as it afterward happened, several men or messes, of six men each, would form a company, dividing equally the skins.

The equippment of each man was rifle & six traps. Besides this, most of the men had a pistol, knife, & a small hatchet that could be conveniently carried in the belt. We had the best of ammunition. For convenience in cooking, &, we were divided into messes of six men each, one of each mess usually volunteering to do the cooking, prefering it to hunting, & he of course received his share of pelts. My equippment [equipment] was the same as that of the others excepting my pistol which I had ordered made. It was the same bore as my rifle, & it would kill a buffalo at 40 yds. Our intention was, when we set out, to be gone about a year or 18 months. We left the vicinity of
in May 1830, & followed up the north fork of
. We had procured a map & with its aid we made our way with little trouble.
When we started we had, each two pack animals & in some cases of us three, so that we travelled slowly, and, besides, frequent freshets kept us back. About the first of July we reached the
But just here I may as well give you the names of those in our company that I can remember. First came

our Captain, whom we had selected in this because he had always borne a good name among us & was very much esteemed rather than from any superior skill or knowledge he possessed in affairs relating to the control & management of such a [an] expedition. Besides being older than most of us. He was accompanied by
, a young man about 24 yrs. of age.
Two men by [the] name of Green
, a large powerful man; he would probably weigh at least 240 lbs;
, who was afterwards treacherously killed by
, who I think is still living in [L...
, of whom I shall have more to say hereafter;
. These are all the names I can recollect at present, but in the course of my story will no doubt be able to recall those of some of the others.
, making very slow marches. About the latter part of Aug. or first of Sept., having marched some 200 to 250 miles since leaving the
,we fell in with a herd of
...that night, six of us started out one afternoon to hunt buffalo. We had been for several days travelling in a country frequented by the

, (principally,) but had seen no signs of them, & had taken no other precautions on the march than to keep close together[,] sometimes riding by twos & sometimes four abreast when the country was open & level. No one was especially detailed to keep a lookout, for the air was so clear that objects could be seen at a very great distance. I frequently saw buffalo as far as 9 mile[s] away, making them out without any difficulty. I have never been in any place since, nor before, where the air seemed so remarkably clear. Then we always rode close together so that within fifteen minutes after an alarm we could have been prepared to receive an enemy. At night, too, ever since entering the indian [Indian] country, we formed our packs into a circular breastwork, with our horses picketed inside, & a guard of from three to five, sometimes more, relieved every four (4) hours, throughout the night. Our party of six, having left the camp, divided separated; three keeping in close to the edge of the belt of timber that ran along the river, the other three, & among them myself, taking the open & higher ground some distance from & opposite the first three. We rode along for some distance in our relative positions, without discovering any buffalo. At last the party skirting the timber saw some horse[s] in the distance, grazing near the edge of the timber, & with the hopes of capturing them, for they naturally thought them wild horses, they approached them under cover. Their surprise as well as alarm, however, can be imagined when, upon nearing the animals the[y] discovered them to be tied. They immediately started at full speed towards us & hardly five minutes after, two

[sic] emerged from the timber, on horseback, & rode after our companions. Following these came others until they were, I should judge, about forty altogether. We ran & they folled [followed] us, and, being better mounted than we were, soon came within shooting distance. They discharged a shower of arrows at us, many of them going beyond us. We saw that it was useless to run any farther so [we] stood our ground or [...] rather rode leisurely towards camp. They did not offer to attack us, but one of them, more daring than the rest, charged down on us alone & rode near enough to throw his lance, which was easily dodged. We supposed that he would ride directly by us & for us and as shots are always uncertain as an when fired at an object in such rapid motion we would have let him pass, but instead of doing this he rode off at right angles to our party, in a perfectly straight line, &, loth [loathe] to lose such a chance I drew bead on him & tumblybled him out of his saddle, & some of our boys caught his horse. This brought forth a yell from
[sic], who, however, did not trouble us any more. Upon reaching camp we wanted to take half of our men & go back & attack

refused, to the great dissatisfaction of all of they party who from his ways had already lost some confidence in him & now they suspected, & with reason as will be seen hereafter, that he was not over brave.
At this junction ten (10) of our party left us, declaring their intention to go back to
, as they feared to go into indian [Indian] country with
as Capt. I have never heard what became of them.
[sic] we decided a day or two after to leave
. Our course had lain chiefly along the North bank of
. The country we had come through was very fine, the land rich, well timbered, especially near

, & game in abundance. We travelled very leisurely, making on an average from 15 to 20 miles a day striking camp about 7 or 8 o'clock in the morning usually' & pitching camp again all the way from 3 to 5 in the afternoon. During the early part of our journey we were frequently delayed by swollen streams, & occasionally by the illness of some of the men, which would make it necessary to halt a day or two.
As had been decided, we went over to the
& having reached it crossed to its Northern side.
we intended to follow until we should reach
A day or two later, in the early part of the afternoon we came upon several

warriors, who came out from the ftimber to meet our party, & others joined us as we marched along until they numbered all told about 80. About half of them were mounted. They were a war party as they had no women with them; a few had rifle[s] but the majority had only bows & arrows. They were fine looking

[sic], about the medium height but well made & active. Their dress was the customary buckskin leggings & the breech clout; a few had shirts. Upon first seeing us they made signs of friendship, but although we considered them friendly
[sic] we did not place much confidence in their good will.
however was very generous with them, making them presents of blankets, tobacco, & even knifves & powder, which the most of our party very much disapproved of.
It must have been about 2 o'clock in the afternoon when we met

, & soon after we went into camp. They went into camp also about 60 yds. from us, in the same body of timber. The next morning we moved on & left them, & the next afternoon camped about the usual hour.
Having to do most of the hunting I usually started out as soon as we got into camp, & generally took my brother
with me, while he lived. Sometimes others accompanied me, as a rule either
This afternoon I started out with

We went fully four miles from camp before we saw anything; then we saw the objects of our search at a distance. We were on foot so had little trouble in approaching them but, before we got with in range I saw an indian's [Indian's] head peeping out of a hollow, & I at once saw that we had been drawn into a trap. I at once told
, & we took to our heels at once immediately, with a party of 80

close behind. Two of them only were mounted, but the rest of them were good runners & our chances of saving our scalp[s] was very small. We were out in the open country, the nearest timber being that of the river belt fully a mile & [a] half away, &
a pack of fleet footed red skins
close upon us. We saw at once that our only hope was to reach the timber where perhaps we might be able to defend ourselves.

was a good runner, the best in fact in our party, & had he wished could soon have left me in the rear, but it was not his character to desert a comrade in danger so we kept together straining every nerve to reach the shelter of the woods.

[sic] on horseback of course gained on us at once, & we were obliged to turn alternately & by aiming at them check them for a moment. Under any other circumstances it would have been amusing to see the horsemen make their horses [...] jump quickly from side to side, at the same time throwing their own bodies this way then that to prevent our taking aim on them, when we raised our rifles, but we only realized that each stoppage was enabling those on foot, who were steadily pressing forward, to gain ground. The chase had been kept up nearly a mile, I should judge, when
were so close upon us that we had determined to fire on them.
, although naturally a brave man, was not a little frightened, & was almost exhausted by our long run, so that [...]in his fright he did not see the foremost

[sic] throw down their guns when he turned to fire, but I fortunately did & thus prevented his shooting, as good fortune would have it. Had he shot, he would undoubtedly have killed one & perhaps more, for he would have fought to the last, but however many we might have killed, numbers would have overpowered us, & I would not be here to tell about it nor [would]
have become famous as leader of

[sic] came up to us, they took hold of us & shook us roughly, & finally made us sit down on the ground while they seated themselves in a circle about around us. They lit their pipe & passed it around, holding council for some time, no doubt about what should be done with us. By signs we tried to make them understand that we had slept with 80 indians whites, the night before not far from there [them?], hoping this might induce them to spare our lives. It had the desired effect, for they soon got up & made motions that we too should get up & lead them to our camp. We needed no second bidding but took the direction of camp with
. We soon reached our party, to our great relief &
camped near us. Our men had not suspected anything when they saw us coming in with
[sic], supposing that they were friendly to us. That night we doubled our guard & took every precaution to prevent a surprise. The night before we had done the same to guard against surpris[e] from
& there was good cause for it, for they were prowling around our camp all night, & only the fact that we were on the alert & prepared, prevented them from attacking us.
had gone into camp at about the same distance from us as the
the night previous. I was in [on] guard until twelve o'clock at night, & several times had seen

I told him, & cautioned him to keep a good lookout. It was not long before he saw an

[Indian] approaching his post, whereupon he called out, "who[']s that." His answer was the snap of a gun lock & the flash of a flint. The indians [Indian's] gun did not go off, but Price Price's did, & we heard afterwards that an
[Indian] was killed. At Price's shot, a yell arose on all sides of us & those who were on guard knew that the
, who was also on guard, called out, "Help, boys". We were all on our feet in an instant & our arms, which we always kept at our sides, ready for use. The

[sic] fired a discharge of arrow[s] & a few shots among us, but did no further damage than to wound a few horses. We replied to their shots but as the night was dark we could not shoot with any certainty, for we could aim only when we saw the flash of their guns. We thought the
also fired a few shots among us, but it was not positively known. We had a small cannon with us & having loaded it with 60 bullets we discharged it among it in the direction of
, & succeeded in silencing their fire. We passed the rest of the night waiting for their return, but morning came & they had disappeared, &
left us at an early hour. As a result of the night attack we found that we had lost 7 horses stolen, & as many more wounded. With the beginning of the firing our brave
had been lost sight of, & also a man by the name of
. They became frightened & hid themselves. From that night time
was totally disregarded & hardly treated civilly so that, soon after, when we got into
. with the first train of the regular traders that went back."
was by [tacit?] agreement looked up to as our leader & continued in command until he was killed. He was a good leader although a trifle easy.
[Arapahoes] were all good looking indians [sic], somewhat larger than the

The country we had come through abounded in Buffalo & wild horses. The former roamed in immense herds. I have never seen so many cattle in in [sic] my life as I saw buffalo in that country. Wild horses were frequently captured by shooting them through the neck just above the spine, usually called creasing[.] This would knocked them down when the hunter would catch them before they could get onto regain their feet. The wound heals soon, & with a little training the horse soon became manageable.
Our meat was principally buffalo, although we occasionally killed deer & bear. In the
was an there was abundance of turkeys & of wild honey.
attacked us, we left our camp soon after the
did theirs. Two days' travel brought us to the
, where we remained one night[.] This village contained about 200 or 250 Warriors & perhaps as many women & children. We passed a sleepless night, & were rejoiced to leave
behind the next morning at an early hour. From this point we had but 150 miles to go to reach
. This we accomplished in about a week. Arrived at the foot of
. we found plenty of feed & our party went into camp. Six of us were sent forward to examine the country before before [sic] us, & to search for a passable trail; as also feed for our animals.

Here we saw for the first time the black-tail deer. There were large numbers of them, so that we had no trouble in killing a considerable quantity for their meat, which we hung up to await the arrival of the main party. We slept one night in
. & the following morning started back to camp. On the way, however,
, one of our party, gave out, so that

& I were obliged to remain with him while the other three went to bring our party up. Before night they had returned with the rest of our company & we went into camp near where we had hung up the deer meat. Here too, the feed was quite plenty & it was decided to remain for a few days to allow the animals to recruit. From this place another advance party of six was sent out to cross
& proceed as far a[s] the head waters of
, a man by the name of

, & I, were of the party. We [...] arrived at our journey's end without any adventure & camped in a beautiful valley in which the buffalo abounded, & feed was plenty. We immediately set to work to kill buffalo & soon had the satisfaction of seeing a large quantity, hung up out of the reach of the wolves & coyotes, ready for the coming of those behind.
As soon as this was done two of our party,

, went back to camp to report, while we, who remained removed our camp[...] from the edge of the valley, where we had hung the buffalo meat, about two miles away & up the mountain's side. From this point we had a full view of the valley below, while our camp was hidden from those below, who might pass through the valley. From a small rise near our camp, we could at all times see our buffalo meat. This change of camp was made to guard against surprise from the indians [sic]. In an indian [Indian] country one cannot take too many precautions, & I owe my life many times over to my habitual vigilance & caution in all my movements while in the indian country
. Of the many trappers I have known that were killed in

by indians [sic], a very large proportion of them were careless & imprudent in their habits. When out with a small force, I invariably ate supper & remained until dark in one place, & as soon as night came on moved away some distance to sleep.
We had been in our new camp but a few days when, having gone out on the rise to look towards our meat, I saw a war party of

, of perhaps 80 or more, engaged in taking it down. This decided us to leave that dangerous neighborhood with all possible haste & make our way back to the remainder rest of our party. About half way back we met our companions at a former camp of the war party of the
I had discovered seizing our buffalo meat. They had arrived there but a few hours before & the first objects that met their sight were the dead bodies of
. They were scalped, & stabbed in several places. From these our party we learned that
had arrived safely at the camp informed theym where we were, & started back to find us. We were never able to find out the manner in which they were killed, but from some Mexicans to whom the
sold their guns afterwards, we gleaned the following facts. The party that chased & captured

& me understood from the signs we made that there were 80 more white men back in the direction we pointed[...]. The fear of being punished by these 80 white men, should they kill us, no doubt induced them to spare our lives. When they reached our camp however, & found only 36 men, they at once concluded that the rest were coming up, & sent some of their best runners back to find them. These runners did not return until our party was already in
. They reported having seen no more white men, whereupon a war party was immediately sent out to overtake & attack us. They fell in with
[Crist], who, probably recognizing them as the same that had camped with in [us] before & through whose village we passed, & unsuspicious, travelled along with them, until an opportunity offered &
[sic] killed them. Of the precise circumstances immediately connected with their death, the Mexicans knew nothing, other than that
was a quiet inoffensive man, but little versed in mountain life & with no skill in the use of arms.
, on the contrary, was one of the best men we had in our company[:] active, strong, hardy, brave, & a good shot. He was missed & mourned by all. This sad event led us to turn our course towards
, considering this section to[o] dangerous for our trapping operations.
we found a few beaver, & at this time had collected about 40 or 50 skins.
As the country through which we must pass before reaching the settlements in
was almost destitute of grass for our animals, already somewhat reduced, we concluded to hide some of our traps, & having found a deep water hole, threw in about 60 or 70.

, a small Mexican town & our point of destination, we met the first signs of civilization, in a herd of tame cattle which we saw quietly grazing off to the right of our line of march, soon after we met a band of sheep driving driven by a young Mexican on horse back.

When we struck the head of the band they parted allowing us to ride in among them & without showing any fear at our presence. Not so with their herd herder, whose attention being diverted did not discover our presence until we were quite close to him, & when then with a startled glance he saw only that a party of horsemen were upon him, &, his fright preventing him from distinguishing us. He uttered a yell & put off for the neighboring mountains as fast as his horse could carry him. He took us for a war party of the
& Mexicans were deadly enemies, & although they would trade freely with each other, woe betide the poor Mexican who was caught by
who might fall into their hands.
A few miles farther on we came to a shepperds [shepherd's] hut, its sole occupants an old man & a boy. The old man was the first to see us & he, like the young herded [herder], took us for

He gave us one look & turning ran at the top of his speed to a ravine near by. He was some distance from the hut & had no time to call to the boy. By this time we were close to the hut, when they [the] boy, hearing us, came out, & recognizing us as white men advanced towards us & held out his had [hand], showing great pleasure at seeing us. The old man had by this time reached the opposite side of the ravine where he stopped to look back & seeing this boy among us, & realizing that we could not be

[sic], he came back almost as fast as he had run away. He ran up to us, caught hold of our hands, & could hardly contain himself with joy. He proceeded to kill a sheep for us, & brought us out milk & corn cakes, which we ate with the greatest of relish.
was about 50 miles distant & we arrived there three days later. Here as at the shepperds [shepherd's] hut, we were received very hospitably, & in fact, every where in
, we always met with the same treatment. This seemed to be a great wheat country, & flour was in consequence quite cheap. Upon our arrival at
they brought us a cart load of bread. There were quite a number of flour mills, also, in & around
, quite a large place. The fur traders who came out annually from
had permanent trading posts established here. We went to
very often but made our headquarters at
. Having arrived here our party separated but 14 or 15 of the original company remaining together.
Those who left us here, as far as I can remember, were,—

, who by this time was looked upon by all the company as the most insignificant among us. We had made a great mistake in choosing him for our leader, but the high estimation in which he was held by all, & his rank of Col. of Militia, led us to suppose him the best man. His brothers were well known to my family,
having been with them in the early indian [Indian] wars. They owned the saltworks on
. He was a quiet, sensible young man, with none of his father's cowardice, & was very much liked by all. They both returned to
with the first annual trading train that left
. About 8 or 10 joined Young's party at
. The names of the others I do not remember.
, who was a large man, had imposed to a great extent upon
, who was a small man, by continually constantly throwing his traps into the river & setting his own on Green's beaver signs.
had warned him that, if he continued to treat him in that manner, that he would certainly kill him. To this threat, however,
paid no heed, nor to the repeated warnings of others of the company, that
would carry his threat into execution.
He continued to ill treat him until
Near the place where he killed
, he had set his traps for beaver, when
, having come across them, threw them into the water, as he had repeatedly done before, & set his own traps in their place. Shortly after,
visited his traps and, finding Anderson's, returned quietly to camp, walked up to him, & shot through the heart.
, but nothing was done with him, I believe, & he was allowed to leave the country.
& came to see me about 7 or 8 years ago, stopping at my house for several days.
Among those of our party who were left, were
; & myself; the others['] names I do not remember. At

& then had 3 or 4 children. I was well acquainted with the family, having often visited their house while I was in
a [I] had less acquaintance[.] I was told that he, too, was married to a mMexican woman but I never visited his house. They afterwards removed to

Having secured the services of three Mexicans with their mules, & procured fresh animals for ourselves, we lost no time in starting on our trip. a [A] few days brought us within a short distance of our traps, but we found the snow still too deep on
. At the same time an event occurred that caused us to retrace our steps with all possible haste. This was the presence in the vicinity of a large party of
. We discovered them on a neighboring hill early one evening morning while we were in camp. Fortunately they did not see us, & as soon as [...] possible we packed up & made our way back to
, travelling day & night until we were well out of the indian [Indian] country. The Mexicans who accompanied us were very much frightened when they saw the
[sic] & needed no urging to keep up with us in our retreat. We were somewhat disappointed in our failure to get the traps, as they were very dear at
Having sold the few skins we brought with us on our first arrival, we laid in a few supplies & in March ('31) our whole party now reduced to 14 or 15 men, set out for
we found a valley with beaver & here we remained until we trapped them out. On Sept. July 4 of '31, we returned to arrived at
again with about two packs of skins of 60 each. These we sold in
, at $4 per pound, on an average of $10 per skin. In those days although there was a heavy duty on all beaver skins bro't [brought] into
, no one ever thought of paying them & as in our case, they would be smuggled into
We fitted ourselves out a second time & in Sept. 1831 again started for the head waters of
. Our party had been increased by the addition of 3 or 4 Mexicans, who had been hired by different members of our company. From
, we kept company with a band of trappers composed of French & Mexicans, about a doz. in all. On our way we saw a few
[sic], & had several horses stolen by them. We also saw numbers of the
, who were then very friendly [to] the Pale faces.
our camp was alarmed one night by the appearance in our midst of

Look- [Looking] up the tree, they discovered me & raised up on their hind legs, growling fiercely & scratching the bark off.

We made the dogs go in & followed with our horses a short distance. The bears chased us out into the open ground. We repeated this until I got a good chance & killed one of the young ones. I did not dare attack the old one as my horse was almost unmanageable through fright, he having been chased & nearly caught once before by a grizzly, & beside of my I was still suffering from several bad bruises & cuts in my head & face. I could have shot one of them while I was in the tree, but was afraid that the others would climb after me. About two mos[.] after this affair, I discovered the old one & remaining cub one day eating acorns when creeping upon them I shot the old one first & then the cub. I knew the old one by its having one short ear. Another day having seen fresh tracks, I followed them down into a hollow with a thick strip of willows running through it. I had almost reached them when I saw four full grown bears. I quietly turned about & rode back. They saw me & set up a loud grunting but did not attempt to follow me neither did I care to go after them.

I shot that year, as before stated, 45 grizzlies, not counting those that got away in the brush[,] most of them no doubt fatally wounded but one of my rules was never to go into a thicket if I knew it contained a bear. I killed some very large ones & one in particular whose skin I sold to one of the trading vessels[.] It was much larger than any bullock's hide they took on board.

My mode of hunting the grizzly was this. If I discovered them feeding was I rode as near as possible with my horse[,] keeping always to leeward; then dismounting I tied him with a small cord which he could easily break if the bear got after him. I then approached the bear cautiously & under cover if practicable[,] taking advantage of the ground, timber &c. I would get as near as 40 to 50 yds. & under no circumstances did I shoot at a longer distance than 100 yds. I never shot at the head as unless a ball could be put fairly in the eye or ear, the constant motion of the head when feeding & its shape make the glancing off of the bullet almost certain. The breast is a good place to aim at if the bear is facing you but I prefer the side just back of the fore shoulder. The aim should be at a spot well back of the shoulder, as a bear heart lies much farther back than that of a deer of [or] buffalo. Here a shot is almost sure to drop a bear dead in his tracks, sometimes they will run a few yards, but very rarely do they move from where they a[re] shot. One objection to shooting them in the breast is the danger of striking some of the larger bones against which the bullet flattens. On the side the ribs are the only bones & they are easily broken or even pierced by an ordinary bsized bullet. If I approached them through timber I always looked about me before firing & selected a tree that I could readily climb in case of need. When feeding they are easily approached, by keeping to leeward, as although their scent is extraordinary, they keep no watch; relying no doubt upon their poweress [prowess].

When a she bear with cubs is found, it is best to shoot the old one first & the young ones can be shot at leisure as they will not leave the mother. I never shot at a bear unless I could see him plainly & was within good range & in this way I never missed one. In a thicket a bear has one most decidedly at a disadvantage, & under no circumstances should they be followed, however severely wounded. If a person is cool, a good shot & above all prudent there is but little danger in hunting bears. Most of those I shot this year & if in fact at all times, I killed almost as easily a[s] I could a squirrel. A person who is not a good shot, cool & cautious & has a good rifle has no business to hunt bears. Nowadays with the magazine & breech-loading guns bear hunting is very tame. In my time we had only muzzle loaders & although, by carrying two guns, we had a reserve shot, our chances were poor if a wounded bear got after us. It will sometimes happen that, in passing through bushes, one comes suddenly upon a bear & there of course is [sic] his chances are poor. When a grizzly is lying in a thicket & hears noise, it keeps perfectly quiet until the animal or person is within reach. At this time there was no sale for bear skins [so] that we never took the trouble [to] skin them unless for our own [use] or to make a present to some friend or acquaintance.

shot a she otter off shore. It was brought to land for the purpose of skinning. Inside of her was a young otter, within a few days of being born. The carcass was being haulded down to the water as was customary after taking off the skin, when

vigorously protested against such a waste of meat. Seizing one of the flippers she drew it back on land, where it lay until the stench obliged us to throw it in the water. By this time however she had come to the conclusion that our food was better than this & she so expressed herself in her own rude way by signs. She was very fond of sugar & fin fact anything sweet & showed her fondness for it by smacking her lips. She had evidently known hunger as she sedulously saved every scrap of food & bones; & the latter she would take out from time to time suck them over & over & then put they [them] away again.
When we took her from her hut she was very carefull [careful] to place the seal's head in the basket although it was almost rotten.
The young otter was skinned & stuffed, making a plaything for
. She hung it by a string from the roof of her shelter & for hours at a time would amuse herself like a child in making it swing back & forth, striking it with her hand to keep it in motion.

One day while out hunting I came across her lining one of the vessels she used for holding water. She had built a fire & had several small stones about the size of a walnut heating in it. Taking one of the vessels which was in shape & size very like a demijohn, excepting that the neck & mouth were much longer, she dropped a few pieces of asphaltum within it, & as soon as the stones were well heating [heated] they were dropped in on top of the asphaltum. They soon melted it when resting the bottom of the vessel on the ground she gave it a rotary motion with both hands until the its interior was completely covered with the asphaltum[.]
These vessels hold water well, & if kept full may be placed with safety in a hot sun. When we left
, we were caught in such a violent gale that we were several times on the point of turning back, but we finally got under the lee of
, which afforded us some shelter until late in the day when the wind went down.
As soon as it began to blow

conveyed to us by signs her intention to stop the wind. She then knelt & prayed facing the quarter fro[m] which the wind blowed, & continued to pray at intervals during the day until the gale was over. Then she looked at us & smiled as much as to say, "You see how I have succeeded in stopping the wind!["]
arriving there early the next day.

Upon nearing the shore an ox-cart came in sight when the old woman's delight was unbounded. She clapped her hands & danced pointing the while at the cart & oxen. On landing I found my sons at the beach awaiting my arrival, one of them being on horseback.
Here [Her] delight at the sight of the horse was even greater than that manifested at the sight of the ox-cart. As soon as she got out of

, she went up to it & began examining it, pointing at this part then that & talking & laughing to herself. Finally she pointed at the horse & placing two fingers of her right hand astride the fore finger of her left, she imitated the motion of a horse.

The same day we arrived here
